questions.promptTex: \text{Two angles of a triangle are } 50° \text{ and } 65°. \text{ Find the third.}
questions.explanation: $180 - 50 - 65 = 65°$. (And since two angles are 65°, this is also isosceles.)
All three sides are equal → **equilateral**. (And all three angles equal 60°.)

choices: Only if it's a right triangle
hints: Check the sum against the triangle angle-sum theorem (180°).
questions.explanation: The three angles sum to $100 + 50 + 30 = 180°$, exactly what the triangle angle-sum theorem requires. So a triangle with these angles **does** exist — it's an obtuse triangle (one angle > 90°).
steps: **Recognize the rule.** The triangle angle-sum theorem: every triangle's three interior angles add to exactly $180°$.
**Classify.** One angle ($100°$) exceeds $90°$, so the triangle is *obtuse* — but valid.

questions.promptTex: \text{A triangle has sides } 3, 4, 8. \text{ Is this a valid triangle?}
choices: No — it violates the triangle inequality
Only if it's a right triangle
hints: Check $a + b > c$ for the largest side.
questions.explanation: $3 + 4 = 7 < 8$. The two shorter sides aren't long enough to reach across the gap, so they can't form a triangle. **Triangle inequality violated.**

questions.promptTex: \text{In a right triangle, the two non-right angles must sum to:}
questions.explanation: Total = 180°. Minus the right angle's 90° leaves 90° for the other two angles to share. They're complementary.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two angles of a triangle are } 47° \text{ and } 68°. \\ \text{Find the third angle.}
\text{In an isosceles triangle, the apex angle is } 50°. \\ \text{What is the measure of each base angle?}
hints: Base angles of an isosceles triangle are equal.
questions.explanation: Two base angles are equal. They sum to $180° - 50° = 130°$. Each $= 65°$.
steps: In an isosceles triangle, the base angles (the two equal sides) are equal.
Total of all three angles: $180°$. The apex takes $50°$, leaving $130°$ for the two base angles.

questions.promptTex: \text{An equilateral triangle has perimeter } 30 \text{ cm.} \\ \text{What is the length of each side?}
hints: All three sides of an equilateral triangle are equal.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two angles of a triangle are equal, and the third is twice each of them.} \\ \text{Find the third angle.}
hints: Let each of the equal angles be $x$. The third is $2x$.
steps: Let each equal angle be $x$. The third is $2x$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Can a triangle have side lengths } 4, 5, \text{ and } 10?
hints: Triangle inequality: sum of any two sides > third side.
questions.explanation: $4 + 5 = 9 < 10$, so the triangle inequality fails. No triangle exists.
steps: Triangle inequality: each pair of sides must sum to more than the third.
Triangle inequality fails → **no triangle** with these sides.

questions.promptTex: \text{In triangle } ABC, \text{ } \angle A = 35° \text{ and } \angle B = 80°. \\ \text{Find the } \textbf{exterior angle} \text{ at vertex } C.
hints: Exterior angle = sum of remote interior angles.
questions.explanation: Exterior angle theorem: exterior angle at $C$ = $\angle A + \angle B = 35 + 80 = 115°$.
steps: Exterior angle theorem: an exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two **remote** interior angles (the two not adjacent to it).

questions.promptTex: \text{A triangle has sides } 3, 7, x. \\ \text{Which range of values for } x \text{ makes a valid triangle?}
questions.explanation: Triangle inequality: $4 < x < 10$. (The third side must be more than the difference and less than the sum of the other two.)
steps: Triangle inequality (full form): the third side must be **greater than** the difference and **less than** the sum of the other two.

questions.promptTex: \text{An isosceles triangle has a base angle of } 75°. \\ \text{What is the apex angle (in degrees)?}
hints: Two base angles are equal. Apex = $180° - 2 \cdot 75°$.
steps: Both base angles in an isosceles triangle are equal.
